revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23985667504|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[en:Hikosan Jingū]][[fr:Hikosan-jingū]][[ja:英彦山神宮]][[zh:英彥山神宮]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine located in Soeda, Fukuoka prefecture, Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=November 2013}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Hikosan Jingū
英彦山神宮 | image = Mt.Hikosan.jpg | caption = Hikosan Jingū Grand Shrine at the summit of {{ill|Mount Hiko|en|Mount Hiko|qid=Q3077886|lt=Mt. Hiko|1=Mount Hiko}}. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coords|33.477944|130.926222|display=inline, title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|qid=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}
[[Izanagi]]
{{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} | founded_by = | established = 546 | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Hiko-san, {{ill|Soeda, Fukuoka|en|Soeda, Fukuoka|qid=Q1205805|lt=Soeda|1=Soeda, Fukuoka}}
[[:en:Tagawa District|Tagawa District]]
{{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}} | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Hikosan Jingū'''|英彦山神宮}} is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Soeda, Fukuoka|en|Soeda, Fukuoka|qid=Q1205805|lt=Soeda|1=Soeda, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Located on the boundary between Fukuoka and [[:en:Oita prefecture|Oita]] Prefectures, {{ill|Mount Hiko|en|Mount Hiko|qid=Q3077886|lt=Hiko-san|1=Mount Hiko}} has been venerated from ancient times as a sacred mountain. It was also a center of training for the [[Shugendō]] sect of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. The shrine is located on the Fukuoka Prefecture side of the mountain. The Jō-gu is located in the innermost part of the shrine grounds on the top of Naka-dake, the center peak of the three Hiko-san peaks. The sanctuary is said to have been built in 546. The Hōhei-den, a large lecture hall built in 1616, and the Kane-no-Torii, a bronze Shinto gateway built in 1637, have both been designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} by the Japanese government. ==History== The shrine was originally built in 546 as a center of training for the Shugendō Yamabushi sect of Buddhism. However, the Shugendō temple was abolished by the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto from Buddhism]], introduced after the [[Meiji Restoration]]. Reisen-ji ([[:ja:霊泉寺 (福岡県添田町)|霊泉寺]]), the head temple of the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai Buddhism|1=Tendai}}, was converted into Hikosan Jinja (英彦山神社). In 1975, it was renamed and status elevated to its present name, Hikosan Jingū. It is the only Jingu in Fukuoka prefecture and is the oldest of three original sacred mountains of the Yamabushi. In the former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] it was an imperial shrine of the second Rank or ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]'' (官幣中社). ==Gallery== File:Hikosan Jingu Hoheiden 02.jpg|Hōhei-den ({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}) File:Hikosan Jingu Hoheiden 03.jpg| File:Hikosan Jingu Kane-no-Torii.jpg|Kane-no-Torii ({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}) File:Hikosan Jingu Omotesando 01.jpg|Omotesandō, very steep stone stairways approaching to the shrine. File:Hikosan Kaen Slope car.jpg|Hikosan Kaen {{ill|Slope car|en|Slope car|qid=Q7541349|lt=Slope car|1=Slope car}}, a monorail system run from Kane-no-Torii to Hikosan Jingū, was open in 2005. ==See also== *{{ill|Mount Hiko|en|Mount Hiko|qid=Q3077886|lt=Mount Hiko|1=Mount Hiko}} *[[Shugendō]] *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Hikosan Jingū}} *[http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/hikosanjingu.html Hikosan-jingu Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization Official Website (in English)] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[en:Hikosan Jingū]] {{Commons category|Hikosan Jingū}} [[fr:Hikosan-jingū]] [[ja:英彦山神宮]] [[zh:英彥山神宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11616915}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Soeda, Fukuoka]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
